prompts: What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised machine learning? Give a brief explanation with one example of each.
I just started learning Python and I'm confused about the difference between a list and a tuple. Can you explain it simply?
What are the main differences between SQL and NoSQL databases? When should I choose one over the other?
I have a job interview for a junior backend developer role next week. What are the three most important things I should prepare?
Explain what an API is as if I have no technical background. Use a real-world analogy.

Below is a technical article about Kubernetes. Read it and provide a concise summary covering the main points, key benefits, and any limitations mentioned.

Article: Kubernetes, often abbreviated as K8s, is an open-source container orchestration platform originally developed by Google and now maintained by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). It automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications across clusters of machines. At its core, Kubernetes operates on a master-worker architecture. The control plane, running on master nodes, is responsible for maintaining the desired state of the cluster. It consists of several components: the API server, which acts as the front-end for the Kubernetes control plane; etcd, a consistent and highly available key-value store used as the backing store for all cluster data; the scheduler, which watches for newly created pods and assigns them to nodes; and the controller manager, which runs controller processes. Worker nodes, on the other hand, run the actual application workloads inside pods — the smallest deployable units in Kubernetes. Each worker node runs a kubelet, which ensures containers are running in a pod, and a kube-proxy, which maintains network rules. The benefits of Kubernetes are significant. It provides automatic bin packing, self-healing capabilities, horizontal scaling, and service discovery. It also supports rolling updates and rollbacks, making it possible to deploy new versions with zero downtime. However, Kubernetes is notoriously complex to set up and operate. The learning curve is steep, and misconfigurations can lead to security vulnerabilities or performance degradation. Small teams often find the operational overhead too high relative to the benefits, especially at low scale.

Read the following product reviews for a mobile banking app and identify the top three recurring complaints, the top two recurring compliments, and suggest two concrete product improvements based on the feedback.

Reviews:

'The app crashes every time I try to view my transaction history on my iPhone 13. This has been happening for two weeks. Customer support never responds.'
'Love how clean the interface is. Very easy to transfer money between accounts. But the fingerprint login stopped working after the last update.'
'Transaction history keeps crashing the app. Also why does it take 3 business days to see pending transactions? My other bank shows them instantly.'
'The design is beautiful and intuitive. Best looking banking app I have used. However I cannot log in with Face ID anymore and customer support has not replied to my ticket in 5 days.'
'Simple and clean design, I will give it that. But the app crashes constantly, biometric login is broken, and I have sent three support emails with zero response. Switching banks if this is not fixed.'
'Transfers are fast and the UI is great. Wish they had a budgeting feature though. Also pending transactions take forever to appear.'
'Customer support is completely unresponsive. App crashes on transaction history. Biometric login broken since last update. Three stars only because the design is nice.'

A junior developer on my team wrote the following description of how they understand database indexing. Read their explanation, identify what they got right, what they got wrong or oversimplified, and write a corrected version they can use as a reference.

Their explanation: 'A database index is basically a copy of your table that is sorted differently so the database can find rows faster. When you create an index on a column, the database duplicates that entire column and sorts it alphabetically or numerically. When you run a query filtering on that column, instead of scanning the whole table it just looks at the sorted copy and jumps straight to the right row. This means you should always add indexes to any column you filter or search on. The only downside is that indexes take up extra storage space. There are no other trade-offs to worry about. Indexes always make queries faster and the database automatically knows when to use them. You can create as many indexes as you want without any performance impact on your application.'

I am a solo developer building a SaaS product for small restaurant owners. The product helps them manage reservations, table assignments, and basic customer records. I have a working prototype but no paying customers yet. I have three months and limited budget before I need to show revenue or shut down.

Here is my current situation: the prototype works but has no authentication, no payment integration, and no way for customers to sign up themselves — I manually create accounts. I have spoken to 8 restaurant owners. 5 said they would pay for it. 3 were unsure. None have paid yet. I am currently doing all development, support, and sales myself.

Given this situation, write a realistic 3-month plan prioritizing the actions most likely to result in paying customers. Be specific about what to do first and why.

You are a senior business analyst. Read the following company annual report excerpt and provide: a structured financial health assessment, three key risks you identify, three growth opportunities, and a one-paragraph executive summary suitable for a board presentation.

Annual Report Excerpt: FiscalYear 2023 — NovaTech Solutions Inc.

Revenue and Growth: Total revenue for fiscal year 2023 reached $47.3 million, representing a 12% increase from $42.2 million in fiscal year 2022. Subscription revenue, our primary revenue stream, grew 18% year-over-year to $31.4 million and now represents 66% of total revenue, up from 63% the prior year. Professional services revenue declined 4% to $9.1 million as we deliberately shifted focus away from one-time implementation engagements toward recurring subscription contracts. Hardware revenue remained flat at $6.8 million.

Gross Margin: Overall gross margin improved to 61% from 57% in the prior year, driven primarily by the increasing mix of high-margin subscription revenue and ongoing infrastructure cost optimization initiatives. Subscription gross margin reached 78%, professional services gross margin was 31%, and hardware gross margin remained at 22%.

Operating Expenses: Total operating expenses were $38.9 million. Research and development expenses increased 24% to $14.2 million, reflecting our accelerated investment in AI-powered product features. Sales and marketing expenses grew 9% to $13.7 million. General and administrative expenses were $11.0 million, up 6% from the prior year, partly due to increased compliance costs associated with our expansion into European markets.

Profitability: Operating income was $3.0 million, compared to an operating loss of $1.1 million in the prior year, marking our first full year of operating profitability. Net income was $2.1 million after accounting for $0.9 million in interest expense related to our revolving credit facility. Adjusted EBITDA was $6.4 million, representing a 13.5% adjusted EBITDA margin.

Balance Sheet: Cash and cash equivalents at year end were $8.3 million, down from $11.2 million at the end of fiscal year 2022. The decrease reflects $6.1 million in capital expenditures related to data center expansion and $3.4 million in debt repayment, partially offset by operating cash flow of $6.6 million. Total debt outstanding is $12.0 million under our revolving credit facility. Accounts receivable days outstanding increased to 67 days from 54 days in the prior year.

Customer Metrics: Total customers at year end were 1,847, up from 1,612 at the end of fiscal year 2022, representing 15% growth. Annual recurring revenue (ARR) reached $33.8 million, up 20% year-over-year. Net revenue retention was 108%, indicating that existing customers expanded their usage faster than any churn. Gross revenue churn was 6.2%, slightly above our internal target of 5.5%. Average contract value increased 14% to $18,300 annually.

Market Expansion: We completed our expansion into Germany, France, and the Netherlands in Q3 2023. European customers now represent 11% of total ARR. We encountered longer sales cycles than anticipated in European markets, averaging 94 days compared to 61 days in North America. We have hired six enterprise sales representatives dedicated to European accounts and expect European ARR to reach 20% of total ARR by end of fiscal year 2024.

Product Development: We launched NovaTech AI Assistant in Q2 2023, our first AI-powered feature set. Early adoption has been strong with 34% of enterprise customers activating the feature within 90 days of availability. Customers using AI Assistant show 23% lower churn rates and 31% higher expansion revenue compared to customers not using the feature. We plan to make AI Assistant available to all customer tiers in Q1 2024 and to launch two additional AI-powered modules by end of fiscal year 2024.

Outlook: For fiscal year 2024 we are guiding to total revenue of $54 million to $57 million, representing 14% to 21% growth. We expect subscription revenue to grow 22% to 26%. We are guiding to adjusted EBITDA margin of 15% to 17%. Key risks to our outlook include macroeconomic uncertainty affecting customer IT budgets, potential delays in European market penetration, and increasing competition from larger enterprise software vendors entering our market segment.

You are a legal analyst. Review the following software vendor contract clauses and for each clause: explain what it means in plain English, identify any risks to the client (the company licensing the software), and suggest how the clause should be negotiated or amended to better protect the client.

Contract: Enterprise Software License Agreement — Selected Clauses

Clause 1 — License Grant: 'Vendor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, non-transferable, non-sublicensable, revocable license to access and use the Software solely for Licensee's internal business purposes during the Subscription Term, subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, including the usage limits specified in the applicable Order Form. Vendor reserves the right to modify the features and functionality of the Software at any time without notice, provided that Vendor will not materially reduce the core functionality of the Software during a paid Subscription Term.'

Clause 2 — Data Ownership and Usage: 'Licensee retains ownership of all data inputted into the Software by Licensee or its users (Customer Data). Licensee hereby grants Vendor a worldwide, royalty-free, irrevocable license to use, copy, store, transmit, modify, and create derivative works of Customer Data for the purposes of (a) providing the Software to Licensee, (b) improving and developing Vendor's products and services, and (c) generating aggregated and anonymized analytics. Vendor may retain Customer Data for up to 24 months following termination of the Agreement.'

Clause 3 — Limitation of Liability: 'In no event shall either party be liable to the other for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages, regardless of the cause of action or the theory of liability, even if such party has been advised of the possibility of such damages. Vendor's total cumulative liability arising out of or related to this Agreement shall not exceed the fees paid by Licensee in the three (3) months immediately preceding the event giving rise to the claim.'

Clause 4 — Indemnification: 'Licensee shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Vendor and its officers, directors, employees, agents, and successors from and against any and all claims, damages, losses, liabilities, costs, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising out of or relating to (a) Licensee's use of the Software, (b) any Customer Data, (c) Licensee's violation of any applicable law or regulation, or (d) any third-party claim alleging that Customer Data infringes any third-party intellectual property rights.'

Clause 5 — Auto-Renewal and Termination: 'This Agreement shall automatically renew for successive one-year Subscription Terms unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. In the event of termination for any reason, Vendor shall have no obligation to provide Licensee with a copy of Customer Data and may permanently delete all Customer Data within 7 days of termination. Vendor may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice if Licensee breaches any payment obligation and fails to cure such breach within 5 business days of notice.'

Clause 6 — Governing Law and Dispute Resolution: 'This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to conflict of law principles. Any dispute, controversy, or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration administered by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules. The arbitration shall be conducted in Wilmington, Delaware. The parties waive any right to a jury trial. The existence, content, and outcome of any arbitration shall be kept strictly confidential by both parties.'

You are a customer support assistant for CloudStore, a cloud storage and file management platform. Use only the information provided in the knowledge base below to answer customer questions. If the answer is not in the knowledge base, say so clearly and suggest the customer contacts support. Do not make up information.

Knowledge Base:

Storage Plans and Pricing: CloudStore offers four plans. Free plan: 15GB storage, no file versioning, maximum file size 100MB, maximum 3 shared links active at one time, email support only with 72-hour response time. Starter plan: $8 per month or $80 per year, 200GB storage, 30-day file versioning, maximum file size 5GB, unlimited shared links, email support with 24-hour response time. Professional plan: $20 per month or $200 per year, 2TB storage, 180-day file versioning, maximum file size 50GB, unlimited shared links, priority email and chat support, advanced sharing permissions, audit logs. Business plan: $45 per user per month or $450 per user per year, minimum 3 users, 5TB storage per user, 365-day file versioning, maximum file size 500GB, unlimited shared links, 24/7 phone and chat support, SSO integration, custom retention policies, dedicated account manager for teams over 20 users.

File Versioning: File versioning automatically saves previous versions of a file each time it is modified. Starter plan retains versions for 30 days. Professional plan retains versions for 180 days. Business plan retains versions for 365 days. Free plan does not include versioning. To restore a previous version: navigate to the file, right-click, select Version History, choose the version you want to restore, click Restore. Restoring a version does not delete the current version — it creates a new version. Storage used by old versions counts toward your plan storage limit.

Sharing and Permissions: All paid plans support unlimited shared links. Free plan supports maximum 3 active shared links simultaneously. Shared links can be set to view-only or allow download. Password protection for shared links is available on Professional and Business plans only. Link expiry dates are available on Professional and Business plans only. Folder sharing with permission levels (viewer, commenter, editor) is available on all plans. External users (people without a CloudStore account) can view and download files via shared links but cannot edit files or folders directly. To edit, external users must create a free CloudStore account and be added as a collaborator with editor permission.

Mobile Application: CloudStore mobile app is available on iOS (version 14 and above) and Android (version 9 and above). Automatic camera upload is available on all plans including free. Offline access — marking files for offline viewing — is available on Starter, Professional, and Business plans. The mobile app supports biometric login (Face ID, Touch ID, fingerprint) on supported devices. Maximum file upload size from mobile is the same as the plan limit. If camera upload fails, check that CloudStore has permission to access photos in device settings and that background app refresh is enabled.

Security and Compliance: All files are encrypted at rest using AES-256 encryption and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher. CloudStore is SOC 2 Type II certified. GDPR compliance documentation is available on request by emailing compliance@cloudstore.com. HIPAA Business Associate Agreements are available for Business plan customers only. Two-factor authentication is available on all plans and strongly recommended. SSO via SAML 2.0 is available on Business plan only. CloudStore data centers are located in the United States, European Union, and Singapore. Customers on Professional and Business plans can select their preferred data residency region during account setup.

Billing and Cancellation: Monthly plans can be cancelled at any time. Cancellation takes effect at the end of the current billing period. No refunds are issued for partial months. Annual plans can be cancelled within 30 days of purchase for a full refund. After 30 days, annual plans are non-refundable but remain active until the end of the subscription year. Downgrading from a paid plan to Free is permitted but if your current storage usage exceeds 15GB your account will become read-only until you delete files to get below 15GB. Payment methods accepted: Visa, Mastercard, American Express, PayPal. Invoices are available in the billing section of account settings.

Customer question: I am currently on the Professional plan and I have some important contracts stored that I need to share with an external law firm. They need to be able to view the documents but under no circumstances should they be able to download them. The links also need to expire after 2 weeks. Is this possible and how do I set it up? Also, one of the lawyers says they are getting an error when trying to open the link I already sent — it just says 'link expired' but I only created it yesterday. Why would that happen?

You are a senior software engineer. Read the following product requirements document and write the complete implementation. Include all functions, proper error handling, type hints, and docstrings. After the code, write a brief explanation of the key design decisions you made.

Product Requirements Document: System: User Authentication Service Version: 1.0 Date: 2024

Overview: Build a user authentication service in Python that handles user registration, login, token management, and password reset. The service will be used as a standalone module imported by a FastAPI application. It must be secure, testable, and well-documented.

Functional Requirements:

FR-001 User Registration: The service must accept an email address and plaintext password. Email must be validated for correct format. Password must be minimum 8 characters, contain at least one uppercase letter, at least one number, and at least one special character from the set !@#$%^&*(). If validation fails, raise a ValueError with a descriptive message. Password must be hashed before storage using bcrypt with a minimum cost factor of 12. The service must check if the email is already registered and raise a ValueError if so. On successful registration return a user ID (UUID4) and the registration timestamp.

FR-002 User Login: Accept email and plaintext password. Look up user by email. If user not found or password does not match, raise an AuthenticationError — do not reveal which of the two is incorrect. On successful authentication, generate an access token and a refresh token. Access token must be a JWT signed with HS256, expire in 15 minutes, and contain user ID and email as claims. Refresh token must be a cryptographically random 64-byte hex string, stored with its expiry timestamp (7 days), and associated with the user ID. Return both tokens and their expiry times.

FR-003 Token Refresh: Accept a refresh token string. Validate it exists in storage and has not expired. On success, generate a new access token and a new refresh token, invalidate the old refresh token, store the new one, and return both new tokens. On failure raise an AuthenticationError.

FR-004 Logout: Accept a refresh token. Invalidate it immediately by removing it from storage. If token not found, silently succeed — do not raise an error.

FR-005 Password Reset: Accept an email address. If email is registered, generate a password reset token (cryptographically random 32-byte hex string) with a 1-hour expiry and store it associated with the user. Return the token (in a real system this would be emailed — here just return it). Accept a reset token and new password. Validate the token exists and has not expired. Validate the new password meets complexity requirements. Hash the new password and update storage. Invalidate the reset token. Raise appropriate errors for invalid or expired tokens.

Non-Functional Requirements: All storage in this version uses in-memory Python dictionaries — no database required. The service must be stateless from the caller's perspective — all state lives inside the service class. Thread safety is not required for this version. All public methods must have type hints and docstrings. No external dependencies beyond bcrypt, PyJWT, and the Python standard library.

You are a senior product manager. Below are four separate customer research inputs about a B2B project management tool. Read all four inputs and produce: a prioritized list of the top 5 features to build next with justification for each, a summary of the most critical UX problems to fix, a one-paragraph product vision statement for the next 6 months, and a list of any contradictions or tensions in the research that need further investigation before making decisions.

Input 1 — User Interview Summaries (conducted with 12 customers): Eight out of twelve customers mentioned that they struggle to understand the overall status of a project at a glance. They open the tool, see a list of tasks, but cannot quickly tell if the project is on track or at risk. Several described having to manually count tasks or export to spreadsheet to answer basic status questions. Five customers mentioned difficulty with how notifications work — they describe receiving too many notifications for things they do not care about while missing notifications they consider critical. Four customers said they find the time tracking feature confusing and never use it, even though they said time tracking is important to their business. Three customers said they would find it very valuable to have the tool integrate with their invoicing software (QuickBooks and FreshBooks were mentioned). Two enterprise customers mentioned that they cannot report across multiple projects simultaneously and have to pull data manually.

Input 2 — Support Ticket Analysis (last 90 days, 847 total tickets): Most common ticket categories: Notification settings — 203 tickets (24%); Permission and access errors — 187 tickets (22%); Data export problems — 134 tickets (16%); Time tracking confusion — 98 tickets (12%); Performance slowness on large projects — 89 tickets (10%); Billing and subscription issues — 78 tickets (9%); Other — 58 tickets (7%). Top support resolution time: permission errors average 4.2 days to resolve, the highest of any category. Data export issues often require manual intervention from the engineering team.

Input 3 — Feature Request Votes (from in-app feedback widget, 1,240 total votes across 67 requests): Top voted requests: Recurring tasks — 287 votes; Dashboard with project health overview — 241 votes; Two-way calendar sync (Google Calendar, Outlook) — 198 votes; Better notification controls — 176 votes; Cross-project reporting — 143 votes; Invoicing integrations — 89 votes; Time tracking redesign — 76 votes; Dark mode — 71 votes; Public roadmap sharing — 67 votes; Offline mode — 52 votes.

Input 4 — Churn Analysis (customers who cancelled in last 6 months, exit survey, 43 responses): Primary reasons for cancellation: Switched to a competitor that had better reporting — 14 responses (33%); Tool felt too complex for their team size — 11 responses (26%); Missing specific feature needed — 8 responses (19%); Price too high relative to value — 6 responses (14%); Other — 4 responses (9%). When asked which competitor they switched to: Monday.com — 11, Asana — 9, Linear — 7, ClickUp — 5, Other — 11. Customers who cited complexity as the reason had an average team size of 4 people. Customers who cited reporting as the reason had an average team size of 23 people.
